title: Has anyone here actually tested how much latency impacts AI trading results?
author: Comfortable-Week7646
selftext:
I’ve been going down a bit of a rabbit hole lately with my trading setup, and something that keeps coming up is execution speed. Not in theory, but in actual live conditions.

Most of what I see discussed around AI trading is focused on models, data, and backtesting, which makes sense. But I’m starting to feel like we don’t talk enough about what happens after the model makes a decision how fast that decision actually gets executed in the real market.

I’ve been running a few small tests comparing different setups, and I noticed that even slight delays in execution can change the outcome of certain trades, especially with tighter strategies. It’s not always obvious trade by trade, but over time it feels like it compounds.

Recently, I started experimenting with running my setup on a VPS instead of my local machine, just to see if consistency improves. mainly because it’s built around low-latency trading environments. Still early, but I’m noticing some differences in execution timing depending on how things are set up.

Not jumping to conclusions yet, but it’s definitely making me question how much of an edge comes from infrastructure rather than just the strategy itself.

Curious if anyone here has actually isolated latency as a variable and tested it properly. Did it make a noticeable difference for you, or is it only something that matters at very high frequency?

Would be interesting to hear what setups others are running and whether changing your execution environment had any real impact over time.

comments.author: Traditional_Ear5237
comments.body: I think you kind of answered the question yourself. AI needs to go through inference first, whether it is LLM or traditional ML, this is a slight loss of time while it analyses the data. The closer you get to HFT the less you want to use AI and the more you want to use simple if-else type models because milliseconds matter. And the longer you make the trading timeframe the less it matters. We are running on 1h timeframe and the inference takes 20 seconds (together with all the other code), when we plot real results against simulated ones it is barely half a percent difference and it can both ways + and - because in the time you are entering the price is likely to move equally in both directions.

comments.author: thebigdDealer
comments.body:
latency absolutely matters once you're past a certain frequency threshold. i've tested colocated VPS vs local and the difference is real but mostly for sub-second strategies. for anything with a hold time over a few minutes, your model quality matters way more than shaving 10ms off execution.


TradingVPS is decent for the price, though i've heard Beeks and Vultr's bare metal options can be more consistent depending on your broker proximity. if you're trading perps or crypto stuff, some platforms like markets.xyz have native API support with no gas overhead which removes one latency layer entirely. the biggest gains i saw came from just reducing the number of hops between signal and order, not necessarily raw speed.

comments.author: ZealousidealCarry390
comments.body: For me the impact was huge because I trade N͏Q and E͏S. I went from 2-3 ticks slippage running my strategies at home to zero ticks slippage running the same strategies on ninjamob͏iletrader, their v͏ps is shockingly good
